issue_title: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'numpy'
issue_body:
Hi there. We're working on using pex to build up happy fat little binaries of some internal web services. Often, these web services rely on numpy/scipy/keras, and I've gotten pex to work with a pure 'numpy' dependency in the setup.py - but it's blowing up on scipy/keras install with the title error. At several cumulative hours into researching this and digging through the pex issues log for information, I figure it's time to ask

We're using pex directly, as we already have a full build system in place, and pants would be waaay overkill here. We're relying entirely on setup.py dependency versioning, as we're doing our best to avoid a need for virtual environments, it works nicely with pex, and it gives developers a single point of reference for all deps/requirements so it's easier to work with. 

My setup is real simple, it just has one local package, one local script as an entry point, and the following install_requires, which is basically gunicorn and the numpy stuff in addition to the result of pip install flask followed by pip freeze:

```
    install_requires=[
      'numpy',
      'keras',
      'click==6.7',
      'Flask==1.0.2',
      'gunicorn==19.8.1',
      'itsdangerous==0.24',
      'Jinja2==2.10',
      'MarkupSafe==1.0',
      'Werkzeug==0.14.1',
    ],
```

Note: Keras depends on scipy depends on numpy, but you can swap keras with scipy in the above block and you get the same result either way. 

I've been testing things a step at a time, and I've got it happily churning out pex files for flask/gunicorn apps with a direct numpy requirement. But it seems to lose its mind when numpy is relied on by a dependency, even if I specifically declare numpy in the install_requires before the problem dependency.

I'm running pex 1.3.2 as a self-bootstrapped binary, and all this is happening in a python3.6 venv that only has pip, setuptools, wheel, and six installed. Command is happening in the setup.py directory:

pex . -v -c runserver.py -o app.pex --python=python3.6 --platform=linux_x86_64 --not-zip-safe

Any help would be appreciated. Full error below:

```
pex: Building pex :: Resolving distributions :: Packaging scipy    
**** Failed to install scipy-1.1.0 (caused by: NonZeroExit("received exit code 1 during execution of 
`['/home/jakethedev/.virtualenvs/p36/bin/python3.6', '-', 'bdist_wheel', '--dist-dir=/tmp/tmpcog0upnb']` while trying to execute `['/home/jakethedev/.virtualenvs/p36/bin/python3.6', '-', 'bdist_wheel', '--dist-dir=/tmp/tmpcog0upnb']`",)
):
stdout:

stderr:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 7, in <module>
  File "setup.py", line 474, in <module>
    setup_package()
  File "setup.py", line 450, in setup_package
    from numpy.distutils.core import setup
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'numpy'
```

issue_title: replace BuildGraph's travesal argument, leveled_predicate with dep_predicate
issue_body:
Currently `transitive_subgraph_of_addresses_bfs` and `walk_transitive_dependency_graph` support a kwarg called `leveled_predicate`, which adds a condition for deciding whether to include dependencies of a target in the traversal. It takes the target that is the dependency and the depth from the root of the traversal as its parameters.

One issue with this is that it makes it difficult to implement predicates dependent upon attributes of the relationship between the target and its dependency. For instance, whether the dependency is exported or not.

I propose replacing that predicate with a predicate that receives the current target and the dependency. This way we can use attributes of either target to determine whether to include the dependency.


Concern: How can you still support intransitive if you don't know what depth away from the root target you are at?

 I can reimplement intransitive support by keeping a lookup table of the root targets and checking the current target to see if it is a root or not when evaluating whether to keep an intransitive target. The one issue with this is that it can change the traversal order when there are multiple roots.

issue_title: Swagger DefinitionBody Error: map keys must be strings; received numeric [200] instead
issue_body:
<!--

Before reporting a new issue, make sure we don't have any duplicates already open or closed by
searching the issues list. If there is a duplicate, re-open or add a comment to the
existing issue instead of creating a new one. If you are reporting a bug,
make sure to include relevant information asked below to help with debugging.

## GENERAL HELP QUESTIONS ##
Github Issues is for bug reports and feature requests. If you have general support
questions, the following locations are a good place:

- Post a question in StackOverflow  with "aws-sam" tag

-->

**Description:**

<!-- Briefly describe the problem you are facing -->
Transformation to cloudformation template converts '200' to 200 causing an invalid template error.

Inside my serverless.template.yaml file the following is transformed into an invalid cloudformation segment.

responses:
  '200':
    description: "200 response"
    schema:
      $ref: "#/definitions/Empty"
    headers:
      Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
        type: "string"
      Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
        type: "string"
      Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
        type: "string"

becomes 

responses:
  200:  
    description: 200 response
    schema:
      $ref: '#/definitions/Empty'
    headers:
      Access-Control-Allow-Origin:
        type: string
      Access-Control-Allow-Methods:
        type: string
      Access-Control-Allow-Headers:
        type: string
**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. add the following Resource to SAM yaml template and try to deploy it using the sam cli:

RestApi:
  Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
  Properties:
    StageName: prod
    DefinitionBody:
      swagger: "2.0"
      info:
        version: "2018-04-18T18:37:10Z"
        title: "MyTitle"
      host: "${myurl}.execute-api.${region}.amazonaws.com"
      basePath: "/prod"
      schemes:
      - "https"
      paths:
        /:
          post:
            produces:
            - "application/json"
            responses:
              '200':
                description: "200 response"
                schema:
                  $ref: "#/definitions/Empty"
          options:
            consumes:
            - "application/json"
            produces:
            - "application/json"
            responses:

issue_title: Change behavior %%current_control_set%%
issue_body: `%%current_control_set%%` now maps to `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\ControlSet001` however this should be ControlSet001. The expansion should be used as `HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\%%current_control_set%%` to match the behavior of the OS more closely.

issue_title: Any plans to port this to Python 3?
suggestion: make INSTALLED_APPS explicitly list disabled apps
issue_body:
(idea courtesy of @christianmemije - cc @rtibbles I know this relates to stuff you're working on)

currently INSTALLED_APPS is a list of names, e.g.:

```
  "INSTALLED_APPS": [
    "kolibri.plugins.management",
    "kolibri.plugins.learn",
    "kolibri.plugins.document_pdf_render",
    "kolibri.plugins.html5_app_renderer",
    "kolibri.plugins.media_player",
    "kolibri.plugins.setup_wizard",
    "kolibri.plugins.coach",
    "kolibri.plugins.style_guide",
    "kolibri_exercise_perseus_plugin",
    "kolibri_instant_schools_plugin"
  ],
```

The problem is that this is ambiguous - if something isn't in the list, is it because it's disabled or because it didn't previously exist?

A more explicit form would include disabled apps:

```
  "INSTALLED_APPS": [
    "kolibri.plugins.management",
    "kolibri.plugins.learn",
    "kolibri.plugins.document_pdf_render",
    "kolibri.plugins.html5_app_renderer",
    "kolibri.plugins.media_player",
    "kolibri.plugins.setup_wizard",
    "kolibri.plugins.coach",
    "kolibri.plugins.style_guide",
    "kolibri_exercise_perseus_plugin",
    "kolibri_instant_schools_plugin"
  ],
  "DISABLED_APPS": [
    "kolibri.plugins.user"
  ],
```

No more auto-re-installing the `user` plugin!

issue_title: Adding "indent" param to AbstractMISP.to_json()
issue_body:
I keep getting the error `TypeError: Object of type 'MISPObject' is not JSON serializable` when running `print(json.dumps(misp_object, indent=2))`. Looks like there isn't a way to do a json.dumps() call on any MISPObject instances if I want to add extra indentation for prettier printing, I'll need to roll my own method instead (example below). It might be nice to have this functionality baked into the AbstractMISP class's to_json() function with an optional "indent" parameter. 

@Rafiot do you think it's worth it to add that in for an admittedly minor use case. If so, I can submit a PR.

Example:
```
    import copy
    misp_json = copy.deepcopy(misp_object)
    misp_json = misp_json.to_dict()
    for i, attr in enumerate(misp_json["Attribute"]):
        misp_json["Attribute"][i] = attr.to_dict()
    print(json.dumps(misp_json, indent=2))
```

Proposal:
```
print(misp_object.to_json(indent=2))
```

issue_title: add-path only works in debug mode
[Bug] Multiple IPv6 MPREACH attributes in one UPDATE with group-updates true
issue_body:
Hi Thomas

I am using ExaBGP for a customer project and would like to express my gratitude at this point. ExaBGP does a great job when it comes to injecting routes into BGP. Thanks a lot for all your efforts!

**Observed behavior**
Recently, our customer started using IPv6 and injecting a first /128 route using ExaBGP. Everything worked well. Then the customer started to inject two more /128 routes with the same next-hop IPv6 address. Still, everything worked well.
Finally, the customer started setting different next-hops for each route and suddenly, the neighbor device kept learning just one route while simply ignoring the other two. I suspected this to be a bug in the firmware of the neighbor device and started digging.

**Long story short**
Setting "group-updates true" - which, according to the log will become the default in ExaBGP soon - leads to an incorrect update message sent by ExaBGP.
### ExaBGP Version

```
root@exabgp-test:~# exabgp --version
ExaBGP : 3.4.16
Python : 2.7.12 (default, Jul  1 2016, 15:12:24)  [GCC 5.4.0 20160609]
Uname  : #55-Ubuntu SMP Thu Aug 11 18:01:55 UTC 2016
```
### Example Config

```
group example {
 router-id <A.B.C.D>;
 local-as 65002;
 peer-as 65001;
 md5 ExamplePassword123;
 hold-time 180;
 group-updates true;

 neighbor <neighbor-ipv6-address> {
   description "IPv6 eBGP session with example";
   local-address <exabgp-ipv6-address>;

   family {
     ipv6 unicast;
   }

   static {
     route 2001:db8::1/128 next-hop <nexthop1-ipv6-address>;
     route 2001:db8::2/128 next-hop <nexthop2-ipv6-address>;
   }
 }
}
```
### With group-updates enabled (group-updates true)

```
IP6 (flowlabel 0x93575, hlim 64, next-header TCP (6) payload length: 156) <exabgp-ipv6-address>.45712 > <neighbor-ipv6-address>.179: Flags [P.], cksum 0xbdb4 (correct), seq 65:181, ack 65, win 225, options [nop,nop,...], length 116: BGP, length: 116
    Update Message (2), length: 116
      Origin (1), length: 1, Flags [T]: IGP
      AS Path (2), length: 4, Flags [T]: 65002
      Multi-Protocol Reach NLRI (14), length: 38, Flags [O]: 
        AFI: IPv6 (2), SAFI: Unicast (1)
        nexthop: <nexthop1-ipv6-address>, nh-length: 16, no SNPA
          2001:db8::1/128
      Multi-Protocol Reach NLRI (14), length: 38, Flags [O]: 
        AFI: IPv6 (2), SAFI: Unicast (1)
        nexthop: <nexthop2-ipv6-address>, nh-length: 16, no SNPA
          2001:db8::2/128
```
### With group-updates disabled (group-updates false)

```
IP6 (flowlabel 0xa3adf, hlim 64, next-header TCP (6) payload length: 115) <exabgp-ipv6-address>.44929 > <neighbor-ipv6-address>.179: Flags [P.], cksum 0x9436 (correct), seq 65:140, ack 65, win 225, options [nop,nop,...], length 75: BGP, length: 75
    Update Message (2), length: 75
      Origin (1), length: 1, Flags [T]: IGP
      AS Path (2), length: 4, Flags [T]: 65002
      Multi-Protocol Reach NLRI (14), length: 38, Flags [O]: 
        AFI: IPv6 (2), SAFI: Unicast (1)
        nexthop: <nexthop1-ipv6-address>, nh-length: 16, no SNPA
          2001:db8::1/128
[...]
IP6 (flowlabel 0xa3adf, hlim 64, next-header TCP (6) payload length: 115) <exabgp-ipv6-address>.44929 > <neighbor-ipv6-address>.179: Flags [P.], cksum 0x133e (correct), seq 140:215, ack 65, win 225, options [nop,nop,...], length 75: BGP, length: 75
    Update Message (2), length: 75
      Origin (1), length: 1, Flags [T]: IGP
      AS Path (2), length: 4, Flags [T]: 65002
      Multi-Protocol Reach NLRI (14), length: 38, Flags [O]: 
        AFI: IPv6 (2), SAFI: Unicast (1)
        nexthop: <nexthop2-ipv6-address>, nh-length: 16, no SNPA
          2001:db8::2/128
```
### Important RFCs

RFC 4271 (BGP-4), section 6.3 states:

> If any attribute appears more than once in the UPDATE message, then
> the Error Subcode MUST be set to Malformed Attribute List.

RFC 7606 (Revised Error Handling for BGP), section 3.g. states:

> If the MP_REACH_NLRI attribute or the MP_UNREACH_NLRI [RFC4760]
> attribute appears more than once in the UPDATE message, then a
> NOTIFICATION message MUST be sent with the Error Subcode
> "Malformed Attribute List".  If any other attribute (whether
> recognized or unrecognized) appears more than once in an UPDATE
> message, then all the occurrences of the attribute other than the
> first one SHALL be discarded and the UPDATE message will continue
> to be processed.
### Conclusion

It appears that both the neighbor device as well as ExaBGP are not entirely following the RFCs:
- ExaBGP should not have multiple MPREACH attributes in the same UPDATE message.
- The neighbor device should return a "Malformed Attribute List" Error instead of processing the update message and discarding (some of) the routes.

May I kindly ask you to take a quick look at this and let me know if you agree? I have advised the customer to set "group-updates false" for the time being, so we are in no hurry at all.

Let me know if you need anything else from me.

Cheers,
Manuel

issue_title: Closing stdin on a Running Container
issue_body:
I am using docker version 2.1.0.

When starting a container the parameter `stdin_open` can be used to prepare the stdin socket for writing.  I have had success doing this with code along these lines,
```
stdin_data = 'some data to send to stdin'

container = client.containers.run(image, detach=True, stdin_open=True)

socket = container.attach_socket(params={'stdin': 1, 'stream': 1})
os.write(socket.fileno(), stdin_data.encode())
socket.close()

exit_code = container.wait()
```

I have also found that this approach can easily hang indefinitely, if the underlying container is waiting for input.  The reason seems to be be that the socket status is independent of the `stdin_open` parameter, so the code running in the container never receives an EOF-like sentinel on stdin.

Any suggestions on how to address this?

issue_title: Empty cells wrongly inferred as geojson
issue_body:
Consider the following source and code:

``` python
from jsontableschema import Table

source = [{'id': '1', 'name': 'john'},
          {'id': '2', 'name': ''},
          {'id': '3', 'name': ''},
          {'id': '4', 'name': ''},
          {'id': '5', 'name': 'ann'}]

table = Table(source)

print([f.type for f in table.schema.fields])

# Outputs `['integer', 'geojson']`

```

It's not a GeoJSON type issue per se, it just returns the last item on the [`_get_available_types()`](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/jsontableschema-py/blob/76b34498644c80b6d3fadc3e4432d0935ebbbbff/jsontableschema/infer.py#L151:L168) list. It looks like calling the [`test()`](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/jsontableschema-py/blob/76b34498644c80b6d3fadc3e4432d0935ebbbbff/jsontableschema/infer.py#L106) method on all types with an empty string will return True (so when resolving the counts there will be 2 `strings` and 3 `geojson`). I don't know the workings of the library so I don't know if returning True on `test()` is expected, but in any case it looks like we could handle empty values separately:

``` diff
diff --git a/jsontableschema/infer.py b/jsontableschema/infer.py
index b38b490..905625f 100644
--- a/jsontableschema/infer.py
+++ b/jsontableschema/infer.py
@@ -102,6 +102,8 @@ class _TypeGuesser(object):
         self.type_options = type_options or {}

     def cast(self, value):
+        if not value:
+            return ('string', 'default')
         for _type in reversed(self._types):
             result = _type(self.type_options.get(_type.name, {})).test(value)
             if result:
```

This needs more thought by someone more familiar with the library (eg do we need to distinguish between `''` and `None`, is `string` the best default, etc) but you get the idea.

Happy to submit a PR with the preferred approach with tests if you point me to the right place.

With the following recipe:

```yaml
package:
  name: multiple-feature-test
  version: 1.0

build:
  number: 0
  script: touch $PREFIX/hithere
  track_features:
    - feat_a
    - feat_b

test:
  commands:
    - cat $PREFIX/hithere
```

the package cannot be installed because it tracks multiple features. The failure mode is an infinite hang trying to solve dependencies during install. Debug logging shows this:

```
DEBUG:conda.plan:Pinned specs=[]
DEBUG:conda.resolve:Checking satisfiability of current install
DEBUG:conda.resolve:Checking if the current environment is consistent
Solving package specifications: INFO:stdoutlog:Solving package specifications:

DEBUG:conda.logic:Eliminating 2/2 terms for bound violation

DEBUG:conda.logic:Bisection attempt: (1,1), (1+0) clauses
DEBUG:conda.logic:Bisection failure, new range=(2,1)
DEBUG:conda.logic:Bisection attempt: (2,1), (1+0) clauses
DEBUG:conda.logic:Bisection failure, new range=(2,1)
DEBUG:conda.logic:Bisection attempt: (2,1), (1+0) clauses

repeating the last two lines forever. I don't really understand the SAT solver stuff, but I'm guessing that when `lo > hi`, it's not going to work and should abort.

Eliminate tracking of either feature and it works.

issue_title: `conda clean -p` partially deletes root Python installation on 32 bit Windows
issue_body:
There appears to be an issue with how `conda clean -p` detects whether or not a package is in use. It attempts to delete the root Python installation in the `pkgs` directory even though it is in use and then silently ignores file locking issues that come up when a file handle is held by a running process. Installing an older bugfix release and then reinstalling the latest one can be used to restore the root install.

We're seeing this with Python 2.7.11 build 2 on Windows Server 2008 and Windows 8.

```
I can circumvent the exception by using the following diff.  I don't propose that this is the solution (I don't know the code well enough), but I hope it's a useful starting point:
```diff
diff --git a/conda/plan.py b/conda/plan.py
index a7fd836..519dedf 100644
--- a/conda/plan.py
+++ b/conda/plan.py
@@ -412,7 +412,7 @@ def install_actions(prefix, index, specs, force=False, only_names=None,
 
     pkgs = r.install(specs, [d + '.tar.bz2' for d in linked], update_deps=update_deps)
     for fn in pkgs:
-        dist = fn[:-8]
+        dist = fn[:fn.rindex('.tar.bz2')]
         name = install.name_dist(dist)
         if not name or only_names and name not in only_names:
             continue
```
The reason this fix works is that the filename, `fn`, is `anaconda-2.1.0-np19py27_0.tar.bz2[mkl]`, and the code assumes it ends in `.tar.bz2`.

issue_title: constructor does not work with master
issue_body:
Using `conda version: 4.0.7.dev432+5503970`, and having the following channels in `construct.yaml`:
```
channels:
  - http://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/
  - https://conda.anaconda.org/ilan

specs:
  - python
  - grin
  - sample
```
I get the traceback:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/ilan/python/bin/constructor", line 6, in <module>
    exec(compile(open(__file__).read(), __file__, 'exec'))
  File "/Users/ilan/constructor/bin/constructor", line 6, in <module>
    sys.exit(main())
  File "/Users/ilan/constructor/constructor/main.py", line 146, in main
    verbose=opts.verbose)
  File "/Users/ilan/constructor/constructor/main.py", line 84, in main_build
    create(info)
  File "/Users/ilan/constructor/constructor/shar.py", line 97, in create
    t.add(join(info['_download_dir'], fn), 'pkgs/' + fn)
  File "/Users/ilan/python/lib/python2.7/tarfile.py", line 2006, in add
    tarinfo = self.gettarinfo(name, arcname)
  File "/Users/ilan/python/lib/python2.7/tarfile.py", line 1878, in gettarinfo
    statres = os.lstat(name)
OSError: [Errno 2] No such file or directory: '/Users/ilan/.conda/constructor/osx-64/ilan::sample-0.6.0-xyz.tar.bz2'
```
To solve this problem, I suggest that `conda.fetch.fetch_index` should have an optional argument `dist_as_key`, where the default is `False`.  The resulting index has the following keys:
  * dist_as_index=False   filename as key, e.g. `sample-0.6.0-0.tar.bz2`
  * dist_as_index=True    distribution as index key, e.g. `ilan::sample-0.6.0-0.tar.bz2`

When the function is called within conda, it can always we called using `dist_as_index=True`.
In this way, the function will behave in the old way for existing code which uses it.

CC @mcg1969

issue_title: LROs fail when a PATCH returns a 201 with location header
issue_body:
From https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/issues/1243

PATCH operations can return 201 with a location header or azure async operation response. However, if they do the following code will result in a failure being thrown: https://github.com/Azure/msrestazure-for-python/blob/master/msrestazure/azure_operation.py#L141-L145

This needs to be updated to also consider PATCH+201 as a success case

issue_title: session.get breaks up certain strings in params
issue_body:
## Long story short

When strings in the params contains semicolons, the string is not parsed into the URL correctly.

## Expected behaviour

with `params={'mode':'fastest;car;traffic:enabled'}`, one expects the url to be `...?mode=fastest;car;traffic:enabled`

## Actual behaviour

The url from response.url is `...?mode=fastest&car=&traffic:enabled=`

## Steps to reproduce
```
async with session.get(url ,params={'mode':'fastest;car;traffic:enabled'}) as response:
        print(response.url)
```
## Your environment

Windows 10, Anaconda 4.4.0, Python 3.5.3, jupyter 4.3.0
This issue was encountered with the HERE routing API.

issue_title: Whatever returned from model validation methods gets disregarded
issue_body: According to docs / examples, model-level validation methods return ["clean" values](http://schematics.readthedocs.org/en/latest/usage/validation.html#model-level-validation). But current implementation of _validate_model() [doesn't expect any return value](https://github.com/j2labs/schematics/blob/master/schematics/validate.py#L82), so whatever returned from model validation methods gets disregarded.

issue_title: List of things to improve with pulsar
issue_body:
[TL;DR]

- the pip version doesn't find the shell_plugins
- connection errors could be more obvious
- parameters from job_conf.xml.sample_advanced apply to app.yml as well [missing doc]
- check_job_script_integrity should be configurable
- mention app.yml.sample prominently in docs, for people that start with pulsar-app installed through pip

----

First of all, I'm amazed at how easy it was to submit pulsar jobs to our university's HPC cluster by ssh, which I didn't know existed yesterday at around this time! Seriously awesome @jmchilton .

The cluster is running PBS, and it is unlikely we can ever become submit hosts directly. There is a fronted server, to which researchers can login by ssh, transfer their data and submit jobs, so I thought Pulsar with a secure_shell plugin and torque are the right choice.

I first tried installing pulsar-app from pip, which gave me version 0.6.1
In the simplest config everything works fine, but once I change shell_plugin to SecureShell, I got this traceback when submitting jobs (server started with `pulsar`):
```
serving on http://127.0.0.1:8913
2016-04-12 14:59:30,536 DEBUG [pulsar.managers.base][[manager=_default_]-[action=preprocess]-[job=33]] job_id: 33 - Checking authorization of command_line [printenv > "/lustrework/vandenbeek/pulsar/pulsar-master/files/staging/33/outputs/dataset_33.dat"]
2016-04-12 14:59:30,537 ERROR [pulsar.managers.stateful][[manager=_default_]-[action=preprocess]-[job=33]] Failed job preprocess for 33:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/marius/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pulsar/managers/stateful.py", line 79, in do_preprocess
    self._proxied_manager.launch(job_id, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/marius/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pulsar/managers/queued_cli.py", line 25, in launch
    shell, job_interface = self.__get_cli_plugins()
  File "/Users/marius/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pulsar/managers/queued_cli.py", line 49, in __get_cli_plugins
    return self.cli_interface.get_plugins(self.shell_params, self.job_params)
  File "/Users/marius/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pulsar/managers/util/cli/__init__.py", line 49, in get_plugins
    shell = self.get_shell_plugin(shell_params)
  File "/Users/marius/.venv/lib/python2.7/site-packages/pulsar/managers/util/cli/__init__.py", line 55, in get_shell_plugin
    shell = self.cli_shells[shell_plugin](**shell_params)
KeyError: 'SecureShell'
```

The current master branch had the same problem..
Then I tried changing into the root directory of the pulsar master branch, and used
paster serve server.ini, and this error went away. So I guess this is an import/path issue.

Then I had problems connecting by ssh, but the error message wasn't very helpful.
```
2016-04-12 15:09:00,613 WARNI [pulsar.managers.queued_cli][[manager=_default_]-[action=preprocess]-[job=36]] Failed to submit job - command was qsub /Users/marius/pulsar/pulsar/files/staging/36/command.sh
2016-04-12 15:09:00,614 ERROR [pulsar.managers.stateful][[manager=_default_]-[action=preprocess]-[job=36]] Failed job preprocess for 36:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/marius/pulsar/pulsar/pulsar/managers/stateful.py", line 78, in do_preprocess
    self._proxied_manager.launch(job_id, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/Users/marius/pulsar/pulsar/pulsar/managers/queued_cli.py", line 39, in launch
    raise Exception("Failed to submit job")
Exception: Failed to submit job
```

Turns out I hadn't added the ECSDA host key to the known_hosts (I typically just ignore them on my dev machine).
I guess when establishing the connection, and there is an error, the error could be returned.

Next thing is that it wasn't very obvious that you can simply translate the resource requests from the job_conf.xml into yaml format. I can add a PR to the docs for this

Then I'm not sure how this part is supposed to work, but for some reason I can't submit jobs when this is present:
```
# The following block can be used by the job system
# to ensure this script is runnable before actually attempting
# to run it.
if [ -n "$ABC_TEST_JOB_SCRIPT_INTEGRITY_XYZ" ]; then
    exit 42
fi
```

I have a quick'n-dirty PR ready to make this configurable.
So now I'm successfully submitting ( have I mentioned how awesome that is?!!!), 
let's see how this works out in practice.

After digging into it deeper, I discovered that `result` contained the following:

issue_title: Inserting a large block is very slow
issue_body:
Inserting a large block into a ProxyList can be very slow because of the _synchronize() method called after each insertion. 

Propose an insert_multiple() method that allows multiple insertions at the same time:
[insert_multiple.patch.txt](https://github.com/PyCQA/redbaron/files/1472419/insert_multiple.patch.txt)

Use of this patch can easily give an order of magnitude speedup for use cases such as loop unrolling that involve a large number of insert operations.

issue_title: Add constants naming rules
issue_body:
`pep8` implies that constants are named in `UPPER_CASE`: https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0008/#constants

Right now this code is considered valid:

```python
# Module-level variable:
delta = 10
```

`pylint` has checks for it: `C:  8, 0: Constant name "delta" doesn't conform to UPPER_CASE naming style (invalid-name)`

issue_title: Research Subnet.discovery
issue_body:
The UI allows you to associate a subnet with a "Discovery Proxy". Here's a screenshot demonstrating the capability:

![screenshot_2015-05-15_12-57-20](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/1012989/7657767/8b6ebfa8-fb02-11e4-96f9-0ab64e5f741c.png)

When you hit the "submit" button, a pseudo HTTP POST request is submitted to `https://<hostname>/subnet/<subnet-id>-<subnet-name>`. Here's a subset of the payload:

```json
{
    "_method": "put",
    "subnet": {"discovery_id": 1},
}
```

"discovery" appears to be a property of the Subnet entity. Can we add this proeprty to the entity? Unfortunately, no. There's a couple big problems:

* HTTP GET responses don't include a "discovery" or "discovery_id" attribute.
* HTTP PUT calls appear to ignore any "discovery_id" attribute that is submitted.
* HTTP PUT responses don't include a "discovery_id" attribute.

What to do? I'm not sure.

issue_title: Configuration option for a custom event loop policy and/or custom event loop class?
issue_body:
It would be useful when testing an application that uses a custom implementation of `asyncio.AbstractEventLoop` to be able to provide a custom event loop policy and/or custom event loop via a configuration option.

It is of course already possible to set the event loop policy globally with `asyncio.set_event_loop_policy` (e.g. in `pytest_configure`) or to override the `event_loop` fixture so that it returns a loop of the desired class, so perhaps this would be unnecessary.

issue_title: Context is cached improperly on nested schemas
issue_body:
I'm running into an issue where passing parameters down into a set of schemas is breaking. I essentially have this:

```
class RelationshipSchema(Schema):
    singular = fields.Nested(OtherSchema())
    plural = fields.Nested(OtherSchema(many=True))
```

I have attempted to set up `plural` with both a `List(Nested(OtherSchema)))` structure and the current `Nested(OtherSchema(many=True))` structure. Both, initially, appear to work and parse. However, within a `@post_load` hook that needs to call `self.context['request'].dbsession` to get a database session, the `plural` fails: it will work on the first call after the services start, but then fail on subsequent calls as it somehow caches that `self.context` object.

My next workaround is going to be to use threadlocals.

issue_title: RFC: Remove `fields` attribute from ValidationError
issue_body:
Currently, `ValidationError` stores the field name and field instances associated with the error.

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields

class MySchema(Schema):
    foo = fields.Int()

schema = MySchema()
try:
    schema.load({'foo': 'invalid'})
except Exception as error:
    print(error.fields)  # [<fields.Integer(...)>]
```

I propose removing the `fields` because it unnecessarily complicates the validation logic.

`fields` and `field_names` were [added](https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/commit/cbc199af077e710f0cd7efd370be86ff857e0d18) at a time (version 1.1.0 😓 ) when it was impossible to access all the error messages for a schema (see #63). It wasn't [until 2.0](https://marshmallow.readthedocs.io/en/latest/upgrading.html#accessing-error-messages-in-strict-mode) that it became possible to get the full error messages dict when using strict mode.

At this point, I don't see a use case for accessing `ValidationError.fields`.

```

The tool currently don't take multiple occurrence keys from input csv  for the `gen` process, but take multiple occurrence keys from input ABOUT file for `inventory` process.

issue_title: Running from python in a conda environment
issue_body:
Hi, 
I've recently installed and started playing with Sacred and I'm trying to run an experiment from inside the python shell.
I'm using vscode default shell (a windows shell) and an anaconda environment where I manually installed sacred (pip install sacred) because there is no conda package available yet.

Here is the small test code I'm working with:
simple.py:

```
from sacred import Experiment

ex = Experiment('Name')

@ex.config
def my_config():
    a = 7
    b = 12

@ex.named_config
def strings():
    a = 'bla'
    b = 'bla bla '


@ex.automain
def my_main(a,b):
    print(a)
    b = b * 2
    print(b)

```
I'm then trying to run it from the python shell with the following commands:
conda activate alon3.6 #name of my environment
```
python  #open python shell
from simple import ex
ex.run()
```

I get the following error message:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Users\aharell\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs\alon3.6\lib\site-packages\sacred\experiment.py", line 208, in run
    meta_info, options)
  File "C:\Users\aharell\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs\alon3.6\lib\site-packages\sacred\experiment.py", line 420, in _create_run
    default_options = self.get_default_options()
  File "C:\Users\aharell\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs\alon3.6\lib\site-packages\sacred\experiment.py", line 407, in get_default_options
    _, _, internal_usage = self.get_usage()
  File "C:\Users\aharell\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs\alon3.6\lib\site-packages\sacred\experiment.py", line 168, in get_usage
    self.base_dir)
  File "C:\Users\aharell\AppData\Local\conda\conda\envs\alon3.6\lib\ntpath.py", line 576, in relpath
    raise ValueError("no path specified")
ValueError: no path specified
```

Any Ideas?

I looked a little bit into it and it appears that the problem occurs because there the default program_name is `None` and `sys.argv[0] = ''`   since I'm running it from inside a shell.

has an empty string as the first input which the causes the error shown above.
Is there anyway around this or do I have to always run my experiments from the command line?

issue_title: Parsing Speed
issue_body:
I have been making great use of this package in my research, but as I recently graduated to 30 MB DX files I noticed the parsing time skyrocketed to the point of being almost unusuable.

Using the %prun command in ipython to load a several MB OpenDX file, I found the following culprits:

```
   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
   149344    2.819    0.000    4.124    0.000 OpenDX.py:746(__tokenize)
        1    0.517    0.517    6.195    6.195 OpenDX.py:694(<listcomp>)
   448021    0.479    0.000    0.479    0.000 OpenDX.py:367(value)
   746754    0.401    0.000    0.401    0.000 OpenDX.py:360(__init__)
   448098    0.357    0.000    4.655    0.000 OpenDX.py:762(__refill_tokenbuffer)
   448049    0.351    0.000    5.199    0.000 OpenDX.py:783(__consume)
   746817    0.308    0.000    0.308    0.000 OpenDX.py:365(iscode)
```

Of these, the tokenizer is clearly public enemy number 1, though the total runtime was 6.24 seconds so the smaller inefficiencies add up to a greater whole.

I think moving the whole DXParser class to Cython is the easiest solution.  This is what pandas does for their pandas.read_\* series of parsers, and they are remarkably fast because of it.

Thoughts on this?  

It may be some time before I work on this, but I expect in a couple months I won't have much choice as my DX file sizes spiral out of control.

issue_title: job.outputfiles "namePattern" and "locations" contain spurious r'\n' in Ganga 6.1.20
issue_body:
I've got some problems with a chain of transforms in Ganga 6.1.20 which runs fine in Ganga 6.0.44.

The first transform in the chain analyses 10 data files in "split" mode, one data file per PBS job.

In Ganga 6.0.44, the job is created by a call to "j = GPI.Job()" and then, if I add a "debug" output in form:
```
trf = self._getParent()
logger.info('trf.outputfiles = %s',trf.outputfiles) # a "debug" output
```
I get exactly what I expect:
```
trf.outputfiles = [MassStorageFile(namePattern='*_validtree_*.root')]
```

In Ganga 6.1.20, the job is created by a call to "j = makeRegisteredJob()" and the same "debug" output gives:
```
trf.outputfiles = [MassStorageFile(namePattern='*_validtree_*.root'), ]
```

Note the additional two characters in the end for Ganga 6.1.20 (i.e. note `'), ]'` in the end). I do not know if they may create any problems later.

All these 10 PBS jobs run fine and the first transform is marked as "completed" in both, Ganga 6.0.44 and Ganga 6.1.20.

The 10 output files from this first transform are then used by the second transform in the chain (via a "TaskChainInput"). This transform creates a single PBS job (which should get all 10 files from the first transform).

In Ganga 6.0.44, I have:
```
job = GPI.jobs(self.active_job_ids[0])
logger.info('job.outputfiles = %s',job.outputfiles) # a "debug" output
```
and, for each of the 10 files, I get exactly what I expect:
```
job.outputfiles = [MassStorageFile (
 compressed = False ,
 namePattern = 'name_of_the_validtree_file_i.root' ,
 outputfilenameformat = 'Subdirectory/In/Which/These/Files/Are/Present/{fname}' ,
 localDir = '' ,
 joboutputdir = '' ,
 failureReason = '' ,
 locations = [] ,
 inputremotedirectory = None
 )]
```

Note the "namePattern" and "locations" above and compare them with what is shown below for Ganga 6.1.20.

In Ganga 6.0.44, this transform runs fine (i.e. it is "completed" without problems).

In Ganga 6.1.20, I have:
```
job = getJobByID(parent.active_job_ids[0])
logger.info('job.outputfiles = %s',job.outputfiles) # a "debug" output
```
and, for each of the 10 files, I get something like this:
```
job.outputfiles = [ MassStorageFile (
   compressed = False,
   namePattern = 'name_of_the_validtree_file_i.root\\n',
   outputfilenameformat = 'Subdirectory/In/Which/These/Files/Are/Present/{fname}',
   localDir = '',
   joboutputdir = '',
   failureReason = '',
   locations = ['/..Full_Path_To_My_Mass_Storage.../Subdirectory/In/Which/These/Files/Are/Present/name_of_the_validtree_file_i.root\\n'],
   inputremotedirectory = None
 )]
```

Please note "namePattern" and "locations" above and compare them to what is shown above from Ganga 6.0.44.

In Ganga 6.1.20, in the ends of both these strings, you can clearly see TWO SPURIOUS CHARACTERS: "\\" and "n" (note: this is NOT the newline character "\n", these are really two separate characters).

That is the reason why this transform fails to run in Ganga 6.1.20 with an error "No input data file given.".
It searches for files which have names which end with these two characters r'\n' (and they don not exist, of course).

Could you, please, think why / what adds these two characters ("\\" and "n") to the file name (when it is passed to the second transform as its input file, after the first transform is "completed")?

issue_title: CREATE TABLE without IF NOT EXISTS fails
issue_body:
%%sql  
     CREATE TABLE bar (
       baz int
     )

This fails with this error:

     An error occurred while calling o56.sql.
: org.apache.spark.sql.execution.QueryExecutionException: FAILED: Execution Error, return code 1 from org.apache.hadoop.hive.ql.exec.DDLTask. AlreadyExistsException(message:Table bar already exists)

The SQL query actually gets executed twice (once to figure out the dataframe has no data, and another time to get the column names).  But it fails to execute the query the second time because the table was created the first time.

You can work around this issue by doing

    %%sql
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bar (...

... but it would be ideal if we could avoid this altogether.

I do not know if it's possible to do queries like `INSERT` in Hive, but this would also affect that by running the `INSERT` query twice.

issue_title: Refactor user and repository CRUD tests
issue_body: The tests in modules [`tests.platform.api_v2.test_user`](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/platform/api_v2/test_user.py) and `tests.platform.api_v2.test_repository` could use some refactoring. They were my first pass at writing tests in Pulp Smash, and while reasonably good, there's a few things that really bug me about their structure. There's repetitive `setUpClass` and `tearDownClass` code, there's a few too many class variables, and the use of numeric indices makes the tests less readable. (See https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/pull/28#discussion_r44172668.) Let's refactor them to hew  more closely to the example in [`tests.platform.api_v2.test_search`](https://github.com/PulpQE/pulp-smash/blob/master/pulp_smash/tests/platform/api_v2/test_search.py).

issue_title: Query with variables
async resolvers in graphene breaks using nested structures
issue_body:
Using python 3.6, linux+mac

I'm trying to fetch data via http but the nested call hangs - this can be replicated by using `asyncio.sleep`

Replication: *comment out `asyncio.sleep` in `fakeFetch` to see the difference*
```python
from flask import Flask
from flask_graphql import GraphQLView
from graphql.execution.executors.asyncio import AsyncioExecutor
import graphene
import asyncio
import sys

app = Flask(__name__)

def Log(message):
    print(f"LOG: {message}", file=sys.stderr)

async def fakeFetch():
    Log('Wait a sec...')
    await asyncio.sleep(1) # Comment this out and things work
    return 'Async World'

class Bars(graphene.ObjectType):
    bar = graphene.String()
    async def resolve_bar(self, args, context, info):
        await fakeFetch()
        return 'Bar'

class Foo(graphene.ObjectType):
    bars = graphene.Field(Bars)
    async def resolve_bars(self, args, context, info):
        await fakeFetch()
        return Bars()

class Query(graphene.ObjectType):
    foo = graphene.Field(Foo)

    def resolve_foo(self, args, context, info):
        return Foo()

schema = graphene.Schema(query=Query)

app.add_url_rule('/graphql', view_func=GraphQLView.as_view('graphql', schema=schema, graphiql=True, executor=AsyncioExecutor() ))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app.run()
```

Query:
```graphql
{
  foo {
    bars {
      bar
    }
  }
}
```

issue_title: Usage with as_manager?
issue_body:
In the [changelog](https://github.com/jazzband/django-model-utils/blob/master/CHANGES.rst) I see:

https://github.com/jazzband/django-model-utils/blob/master/CHANGES.rst#24-20151203

> Remove PassThroughManager. Use Django's built-in QuerySet.as_manager() and/or Manager.from_queryset() utilities instead.

However, if I create a:

```python
class CustomQuerySet(InheritanceQuerySetMixin, models.QuerySet):
    pass
```

With an implementation of:

```
class MyModel(models.Model):
    objects = CustomQuerySet.as_manager()
    # insert fields fields
```

The resulting ``MyModel.objects.all()`` won't return upcasted models.

The reason I use ``as_manager`` is I can just keep one ``QuerySet`` and avoid duplicating stuff unnecessarily.  I think there was an intention to have it implemented this was from a long time ago, but it seems it didn't happen.

Or, I'm wrong, and making a mistake... :smile:

issue_body:
I'm trying to convert tensorflow quantised .pb file to .lite using toco. The command for creating .pb file is :
retrain.py is [here](https://github.com/googlecodelabs/tensorflow-for-poets-2/blob/master/scripts/retrain.py) and [here](https://github.com/tensorflow/hub/blob/master/examples/image_retraining/retrain.py).

--bottleneck_dir=/mobilenet_q/bottlenecks \
--how_many_training_steps=4000 \

--output_labels=/mobilenet_q/retrained_labels_mobilenet_q_1_224.txt \
--image_dir=/data \
--architecture=mobilenet_1.0_224_quantized
```
When I'm trying to convert the .pb file to .tflite using toco command:

bazel run --config=opt //tensorflow/contrib/lite/toco:toco \
  -- --input_file= retrained_graph_mobilenet_q_1_224.pb \
  --output_file= retrained_graph_mobilenet_q_1_224.lite \
  --input_format=TENSORFLOW_GRAPHDEF \

  --input_shape=1,224,224,3 \

```
I'm getting the error:
`Some of the operators in the model are not supported by the standard TensorFlow Lite runtime. If you have a custom implementation for them you can disable this error with --allow_custom_ops, or by setting allow_custom_ops=True when calling tf.contrib.lite.toco_convert(). Here is a list of operators for which  you will need custom implementations: Dequantize.`

issue_title: Can't run vz-line-chart in demos (plugin development related)
issue_body:
I'm working on a plugin for TensorBoard that uses the vz-line-charts and I'm unable to test the work using `bazel run DEMO_TARGET` as I get the following error in the browser:

```
Uncaught SyntaxError: Unexpected token export vz-chart-helpers.js:15
```

As I understand it, the JavaScript generated by the TypeScript compiler is considered a module (it contains top-level exports) and therefore won't work in browsers without a module loader. As vulcanize inlines everything, this problem doesn't show up in the distribution, just in development workflow.

How does Google deal with this when developing components? Even with small components, vulcanize takes quite a while to run and the generated file is impractical for debugging. I'd like a work flow that simply supports a browser refresh after a file edit. Apart from the module problem described here, this works well using `bazel run DEMO_TARGET`.

Any thoughts on how to with with these modules using this (or a similar) dev workflow?

issue_title: Ruby gapic_code_dir is wrong for multiword API titles
issue_body:
### What

Multiword API titles are dash-separated in the artman config (e.g., `video-intelligence`) but should be underscore separated in the Ruby output directory (e.g., `google-cloud-video_intelligence`). Current behavior preserves the dash-separation in `gapic_code_dir` (`google-cloud-video-intelligence`).

issue_title: vectorize() for class member functions?
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm currently trying to make some improvements to the healpy package (https://github.com/healpy/healpy), and pybind11 seems to be an almost perfect tool for the interfacing between Python and C++ that this package needs.
My first experiments went well, but then I hit the problem that the vectorize() functionality of pybind11 does not appear to support class member functions.

Assuming I have in C++

```
class foo {
  public:
    double bar(double arg);
};
```
I'd like to provide a Python function that takes either a scalar or a numpy array, calls foo::bar for all individual values, and then returns either a scalar or a numpy array. (The class object itself will always be a scalar.)
Is there a way to achieve this with current pybind11 versions? Naive application of vectorize() doesn't seem to work, like so:

```
  py::class_<foo>(m, "foo")
     .def("bar",py::vectorize(&foo::bar))
```

I'd be grateful for any tips!

issue_title: More robust pickling support
issue_body:
The current documentation about pickling support states:

> Note that only the cPickle module is supported on Python 2.7. It is also important to request usage of the highest protocol version using the -1 argument to dumps. Failure to follow these two steps will lead to important pybind11 memory allocation routines to be skipped during unpickling, which will likely cause memory corruption and/or segmentation faults.

The cPickle restriction for Python 2.x does not look too problematic, but I believe the requirement about the explicit protocol version is an issue. Forcing the ``-1`` argument in internal use is not problematic if one writes a couple of thin wrappers around ``loads``/``dumps`` for the purpose, but it is fragile with respect to those situations in which one cannot control how ``loads``/``dumps`` are called (e.g., the multiprocessing module, ipyparallel, etc.).

Would it be possible to remove this restriction?

issue_body:
(Note: I know this applies to Nginx, but with my limited knowledge of Certbot I don't know whether this applies to Apache or not)

I have multiple contexts and servers set up in my Nginx configuration, including a `stream` proxy (see #5565) listening on port 6697 and a regular `http` proxy listening on 443. I would like for Certbot to configure both (including the SSL cert, key, dhparams, etc). One of the issues I came across doing a manual copy was that ` options-ssl-nginx.conf` can't be used for more than one server, as you'd wind up with an `nginx: [emerg] the shared memory zone "le_nginx_SSL" is already declared for a different use in /etc/letsencrypt/options-ssl-nginx.conf:7`, so the `ssl_session_cache` would need to have separate entries per server.

issue_title: Swap Tokens functionality always returns success, even when it can't succeed
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

Swap Tokens API and tests doesn't cover when some required pre-condition fails and the tokens call can't be completed. API always returns `201 CREATED`, misleading users on which the call succeeded or failed.

From @hackaugusto, requirements for a token swap are something like:
_Use case_: **UserA** wants to exchange **M** tokens **T1** for **N** tokens **T2** with **UserB**

1. Both users must be participating in both token networks, T1 and T2
2. UserA must have a path through the T1 network to pay for the M T1 tokens, lets call P1
3. UserB must have a path through the T2 network to pay for the N T2 tokens, lets call P2
4. All nodes from P1 and P2, including UserA and UserB nodes, must be online at the time of the operation

## Solution

Ensure that both the `maker` and `taker` calls fails with `404` (Path Not Found) and/or other proper HTTP error if any of above conditions fails, and succeed with `201` (Created) only if everything goes ok.

Tests covering this feature which needs to be updated:
- [tests/unit/api/test_python_api.py::test_token_swap](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ffbb19c5bc26e718af081328eac98e8519b254c8/raiden/tests/unit/api/test_python_api.py#L147-L191)
- [tests/api/test_api.py::test_api_token_swaps](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ffbb19c5bc26e718af081328eac98e8519b254c8/raiden/tests/api/test_api.py#L861-L915)

Test helper functions which may need to be adapted (from `tests/utils/apitestcontext.py`):
- [specify_tokenswap_input](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ffbb19c5bc26e718af081328eac98e8519b254c8/raiden/tests/utils/apitestcontext.py#L66-L77)
- [token_swap](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ffbb19c5bc26e718af081328eac98e8519b254c8/raiden/tests/utils/apitestcontext.py#L272-L276)
- [expect_token_swap](https://github.com/raiden-network/raiden/blob/ffbb19c5bc26e718af081328eac98e8519b254c8/raiden/tests/utils/apitestcontext.py#L278-L283)

## Tasklist

- [ ] Implement all possible checks for all required pre-conditions, and raise proper exception if any fails, with proper error messages.
- [ ] Handle above exceptions in the http API, and respond user with proper HTTP error.
- [ ] Expand swap tokens tests with expected-to-fail testing code.
- [ ] Handle these errors in webUI and show informative messages to users.

issue_title: Batch the writting of state_changes in the WAL
issue_body:
## Problem Definition

At the moment the Write Ahead Log gets its transactions written as they appear. This may not be optimally performant in production

## Solution

Implement a queue of state changes that would be held by the log and each logging action would append to the queue. In certain intervals the queue would be popped and a batch of writes would happen to the log.


## Tasklist

- [ ] Make the WAL keep a queue of state_changes and perform a batch write in certain times.

issue_title: (linear) range normalization? std normalization?
issue_body:
I don't see transforms for linear normalization (e.g. between 0-1 or arbitrary ranges) or std normalization? Any plans for this? Very useful when sampling from folders..

Anyways, here's some code to do these things:


```python
class RangeNormalize(object):
    """Given min_val: (R, G, B) and max_val: (R,G,B),
    will normalize each channel of the torch.*Tensor to
    the provided min and max values.

    Works by efficiently calculating a linear transform:
        a = (max'-min')/(max-min)
        b = max' - a * max
        new_value = a * value + b
    where min' & max' are given values, 
    and min & max are observed min/max for each channel

    Example:
        >>> x = torch.rand(3,50,50)
        >>> rn = RangeNormalize((0,0,10),(1,1,11)) # normalize last channel between 10-11
        >>> x_norm = rn(x) 

    Also works with just one value for min/max across all channels:
        >>> x = torch.rand(3,50,50)
        >>> rn = RangeNormalize(-1,1)
        >>> x_norm = rn(x)
    """
    def __init__(self, min_, max_):
        if not isinstance(min_, list) and not isinstance(min_, tuple):
            min_ = [min_]*3
        if not isinstance(max_, list) and not isinstance(max_, tuple):
            max_ = [max_]*3

        self.min_ = min_
        self.max_ = max_

    def __call__(self, tensor):
        for t, min_, max_ in zip(tensor, self.min_, self.max_):
            max_val = torch.max(t)
            min_val = torch.min(t)
            a = (max_-min_)/float(max_val-min_val)
            b = max_ - a * max_val
            t.mul_(a).add_(b)
        return tensor
```

and

```
class StdNormalize(object):

    def __init__(self):
        pass

    def __call__(self, tensor):
        for t in tensor:
            mean = torch.mean(t)
            std  = torch.std(t)
            t.sub_(mean).div_(std)
        return tensor    
```

issue_title: It should be possible to inspect the global state through Python.
issue_body:
This can already be done (inconveniently) by issuing Redis commands from Python, but we should provide methods, perhaps something like the following.

```python
# This returns a dictionary mapping object ID to a list of plasma manager client IDs.
ray.global_state.object_table()
# This returns a list of plasma manager client IDs.
ray.global_state.object_table(object_id=...)

# This returns a dictionary mapping task to a dictionary of the scheduling state, the
# associated local scheduler ID, and the task spec (already parsed by flatbuffers).
ray.global_state.task_table()
# This does the same but for a specific task ID.
ray.global_state.task_table(task_id=...)

# This returns a mapping from object ID to the task ID of the task that created it.
ray.global_state.object_to_task(object_id=...)

# This returns a dictionary mapping node IP address to another dictionary mapping 
# client ID to information about the client (client type, etc).
ray.global_state.node_info()
```

This probably needs a lot of refinement.

issue_title: Missing some output widgets when interact() calls a function which starts a thread which calls a function
issue_body:
(Opening issue as requested by Jason Grout in the Gitter channel) My code calls `ipywidgets.interact()`, which calls an `alpha()` function. `alpha()` defines two inner functions: `beta()` and `gamma()`. `alpha()` displays a progress bar and starts `gamma()` in a new thread; `gamma()` waits for a timer to elapse and calls `beta()`. If `beta()` is the only function to render ipywidgets or call `display()`, then everything will seem to work correctly. The problem: if `alpha()` or `gamma()` try to render ipywidgets or call `display()`, then the `display()` calls in `beta()` don't always happen. Sometimes none of them add any outputs to the notebook, sometimes only some of the outputs are added, and in rare cases all of the outputs happen just fine. I have not seen any of the `display()` calls come in out of order, however. My code: ```python import datetime import threading import time from IPython.display import ( HTML, display, ) import ipywidgets def alpha(whatever): def beta(something): display(HTML("

One {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Two {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Three {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Four {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Five {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Six {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Seven {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Eight {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Nine {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) display(HTML("

Ten {} {}

".format(something, datetime.datetime.now()))) def gamma(a_thing, timerlength=3.0, updateinterval=0.2): ctr = 0 #display(HTML('

Trying to break it from gamma()

')) while ctr < timerlength: time.sleep(updateinterval) ctr += updateinterval beta(a_thing) streetmap_lock = threading.RLock() #display(HTML('

Trying to break it from alpha()

')) mapthread = threading.Thread( target=gamma, args=(whatever,)) mapthread.start() ipywidgets.interact( alpha, whatever=ipywidgets.Text( value="WHATEVER", description="Whatever")) ``` When I copy and paste that into a Python3 cell as is, I can run the cell and wait three seconds and the ten `display()` calls from `beta()` come through every time. But when I uncomment the `display()` call in `alpha()` or `gamma()`, the ten calls in `beta()` are very inconsistent. (In my real code, I want to display an `IntProgress` in `alpha()` or `gamma()` - it doesn't matter which - and that's when I tripped over this problem. Also in my real code is a global object that I am using to cancel the operation if the user changes a parameter before the timer is up, and restart it with the new parameter value.) Note that the timer is 3 seconds long, which is certainly long enough that nothing is trying to call `display()` at the same time. To be absolutely sure, I tested it with a `threading.RLock` instance that was passed between the functions, and still found the same behavior described above. Tested with: - Python 3.6 - Jupyter 4.3.0 - Jupyter Notebook 5.2.0 - ipywidgets 7.0.3 - widgetsnbextension 3.0.6 I built my test case on the latest version of macOS, but I was seeing similar errors in my real (larger, messier) notebook under Windows and Linux as well. Is this a bug? Or am I doing something wrong? Thanks for any help

issue_title: DynamicRNNLayer without dropout multi layer will error
issue_body:
There is some code error when DynamicRNNLayer with multi layer and without dropout .
code
```
        with tf.variable_scope('rnn') as vs:
            tl.layers.DynamicRNNLayer(
                left_embed,
                n_hidden=self.hidden_size,
                cell_fn=tf.contrib.rnn.BasicLSTMCell,
                n_layer=2
            )
```

trackback
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Users/wuguohua/Envs/py3tf/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tensorlayer/layers/recurrent.py", line 1086, in __init__
    self.cell = MultiRNNCell_fn([cell_creator(is_last=i == n_layer - 1) for i in range(n_layer)], state_is_tuple=True)
  File "/Users/wuguohua/Envs/py3tf/lib/python3.6/site-packages/tensorlayer/layers/recurrent.py", line 1086, in <listcomp>
    self.cell = MultiRNNCell_fn([cell_creator(is_last=i == n_layer - 1) for i in range(n_layer)], state_is_tuple=True)
TypeError: <lambda>() got an unexpected keyword argument 'is_last'
```

solution
```
        rnn_creator = lambda: cell_fn(num_units=n_hidden, **cell_init_args)
to
        rnn_creator = lambda is_last: cell_fn(num_units=n_hidden, **cell_init_args)
```

issue_title: memory_usage returning incorrect numbers
issue_body:
I am using `memory_usage` to profile a large database-reading function with `memu = memory_usage((findNormParams, ('C', db, np.arange(499999), 10)), interval = 1`. The specifics of how my function works isn't important, but that 10 is supposed to be a limit on the memory the database reads can take in GiB.

After careful study of how much memory each read involved takes, I have gotten my function to work within the memory limit I give it. Monitoring total memory consumption by the process in the System Monitor shows the process never takes more than about 9.9 GiB.

However, if I `print (max(memu)-min(memu))/1024` to put the MiBs returned from `memory_usage` in GiB, I get a number closer to 34. If I `@profile` my function, I also see it is taking more memory than it actually is: 29728.5 MiB increase on a for loop! (with no more granular explanation of what inside the for loop is actually taking that memory, a bug I think you fixed in a later version. I should upgrade.)

These numbers can not be correct. Is there any reason for this? I'm using memory_profiler version 0.47.
